meta.question: Could a high-energy particle collider (the LHC) create a black hole that destroys Earth?

phrasings.text: The LHC cannot create a black hole that destroys Earth.

titles.text: Safety answer: no LHC-caused black-hole catastrophe

phrasings.text: Collider-produced black holes pose a real risk of destroying Earth.

titles.text: Catastrophe claim: collider black holes are a real risk

phrasings.text: In standard four-dimensional gravity, the Planck scale is about 10^16 TeV -- roughly 10^15 times the LHC's collision energies.

source.title: Dimopoulos & Landsberg, Black Holes at the LHC, 2001; Phys. Rev. Lett. 87, 161602

titles.text: Standard-gravity Planck scale vs LHC energy

phrasings.text: In standard four-dimensional gravity, LHC collision energies are far too low to produce a black hole.

titles.text: Standard 4D gravity: LHC cannot make black holes

phrasings.text: Large-extra-dimension (low-scale-gravity) theoretical scenarios posit a fundamental Planck scale near 1 TeV, far below the roughly 10^16 TeV scale of standard four-dimensional gravity.

source.title: Dimopoulos & Landsberg, Black Holes at the LHC, 2001; Phys. Rev. Lett. 87, 161602

titles.text: Large-extra-dimension scenarios: TeV-scale Planck mass

phrasings.text: Producing micro black holes at the LHC requires physics beyond the Standard Model, such as extra spatial dimensions that lower the effective Planck scale, since the Standard Model itself predicts LHC energies are too low to create black holes.

source.title: Dimopoulos S, Landsberg G. "Black Holes at the LHC." Physical Review Letters 87, 161602 (2001)

titles.text: Micro-BH production requires beyond-Standard-Model physics

phrasings.text: Microscopic black-hole production at the LHC is possible only under speculative, unconfirmed beyond-Standard-Model physics (large extra dimensions / a low fundamental Planck scale) -- not under standard four-dimensional gravity, and that physics has never been observed to hold.

titles.text: Production needs unconfirmed new physics

phrasings.text: In large-extra-dimension scenarios where the fundamental Planck scale is near 1 TeV, microscopic black-hole production is considered one of the least model-dependent predictions of TeV-scale gravity.

source.title: Dimopoulos & Landsberg, Black Holes at the LHC, 2001; Phys. Rev. Lett. 87, 161602

titles.text: Production is a model-independent TeV-gravity prediction

phrasings.text: If the fundamental Planck scale is low enough (below roughly 2 TeV), the LHC could in principle produce microscopic black holes at a rate of order one per second.

source.title: Dimopoulos & Landsberg, Black Holes at the LHC, 2001; Phys. Rev. Lett. 87, 161602

titles.text: Predicted production rate under low Planck scale

phrasings.text: Giddings and Thomas argued that if the fundamental scale of gravity is near a TeV, high-energy colliders would function as 'black-hole factories,' with black-hole production becoming a dominant process above the fundamental scale.

source.title: Giddings & Thomas, High-energy colliders as black-hole factories: the end of short-distance physics, 2001; Phys. Rev. D 65, 056010

titles.text: Colliders as 'black-hole factories' (Giddings & Thomas)

phrasings.text: Collider black-hole production under TeV-scale gravity is a specific, quantitatively predicted phenomenon -- of order one black hole per second -- rather than an open-ended or vague possibility.

titles.text: Production, if the scenario held, is a sharp quantitative prediction

phrasings.text: Any collider-produced black holes would behave semiclassically only well above the fundamental Planck scale, and would decay by Hawking evaporation rather than persist -- production and rapid decay are part of the same theoretical package.

source.title: Giddings & Thomas, High-energy colliders as black-hole factories: the end of short-distance physics, 2001; Phys. Rev. D 65, 056010

titles.text: Production and Hawking decay are the same theoretical package

phrasings.text: Collider-produced black holes cannot be theoretically separated from their own rapid Hawking decay -- the same TeV-gravity models that predict production also predict near-instant evaporation, so invoking one without the other is not a coherent use of the theory.

titles.text: Production and evaporation cannot be theoretically decoupled

phrasings.text: Hawking radiation is a theoretically well-motivated prediction that black holes emit thermal radiation and lose mass, with smaller black holes radiating more intensely and evaporating faster.

source.title: Hawking SW. "Black hole explosions?" Nature 248, 30-31 (1974)

titles.text: Hawking radiation: the theoretical prediction

phrasings.text: A microscopic black hole would, on standard theory, evaporate almost instantaneously via Hawking radiation -- a smaller black hole radiates faster and is highly unstable, disappearing in a tiny fraction of a second.

source.title: Dimopoulos S, Landsberg G. "Black Holes at the LHC." Physical Review Letters 87, 161602 (2001)

titles.text: Any micro black hole would evaporate at once

phrasings.text: CERN's official position states that microscopic black holes, if produced at the LHC, would decay immediately via Hawking radiation.

source.title: The Safety of the LHC — CERN

titles.text: CERN: micro black holes would decay immediately

phrasings.text: Hawking radiation from an astrophysical or micro black hole has never been directly observed.

source.title: Hawking SW. "Black hole explosions?" Nature 248, 30-31 (1974)

titles.text: Hawking radiation has never been directly observed

phrasings.text: Because Hawking radiation has never been directly observed, the evaporation-based defense against LHC-produced black holes relies on an unconfirmed theoretical prediction rather than an experimentally established fact.

titles.text: Evaporation defense rests on an unconfirmed prediction

phrasings.text: Hawking radiation's unconfirmed status means the evaporation defense against LHC-produced black holes may fail, leaving a genuine gap in the safety case.

titles.text: Risk reading: unconfirmed evaporation may fail

phrasings.text: Despite lacking direct observation, Hawking radiation is derived from well-established general-relativistic and quantum-field-theoretic principles and is treated as reliable by the physics community, so the evaporation defense stands on solid theoretical footing.

titles.text: Safety reading: unobserved but theoretically solid

phrasings.text: CERN's LHC Safety Assessment Group review notes that even setting Hawking radiation aside, the astrophysical cosmic-ray, white-dwarf, and neutron-star evidence independently bounds the risk of LHC-produced black holes to negligible.

source.title: Review of the Safety of LHC Collisions (LHC Safety Assessment Group: Ellis, Giudice, Mangano, Tkachev, Wiedemann), 2008; publ. J. Phys. G 35, 115004

titles.text: LSAG: safety case does not hinge on Hawking radiation alone

phrasings.text: The safety conclusion against LHC-produced black holes does not depend on Hawking evaporation being confirmed, because independent astrophysical bounds constrain the risk even if evaporation were to fail.

titles.text: The astrophysical safety case is layered

phrasings.text: Cosmic rays with energy of 10^17 eV or more have struck Earth's surface more than 3x10^22 times since its formation -- equivalent to Nature having already run about a hundred thousand LHC-equivalent experimental programmes on Earth alone.

source.title: Review of the Safety of LHC Collisions (LHC Safety Assessment Group: Ellis, Giudice, Mangano, Tkachev, Wiedemann), 2008; publ. J. Phys. G 35, 115004

titles.text: LSAG: >3x10^22 cosmic-ray impacts on Earth

phrasings.text: LSAG's overarching safety principle is that whatever the LHC does in its collisions, Nature has already done many times over on Earth and other astronomical bodies during their lifetimes.

source.title: Review of the Safety of LHC Collisions (LHC Safety Assessment Group: Ellis, Giudice, Mangano, Tkachev, Wiedemann), 2008; publ. J. Phys. G 35, 115004

titles.text: LSAG principle: Nature already ran this experiment

phrasings.text: The peer-reviewed RHIC disaster-scenario review found that cosmic-ray collisions elsewhere in the universe have already provided far more numerous and higher-energy collision opportunities than a terrestrial collider does, with no catastrophe having occurred.

source.title: Jaffe, Busza, Sandweiss & Wilczek, Review of Speculative 'Disaster Scenarios' at RHIC, 2000; Rev. Mod. Phys. 72, 1125

titles.text: RHIC review: cosmic rays out-collide any collider

phrasings.text: CERN's official safety statement affirms that the cosmic-ray record shows Nature has already run the equivalent of LHC collisions on Earth and in the cosmos without harm.

source.title: The Safety of the LHC — CERN

titles.text: CERN: cosmic-ray record shows equivalent collisions already run

phrasings.text: High-energy particle collisions at or above LHC collision energies have occurred naturally and repeatedly -- via cosmic rays striking Earth's atmosphere, the Moon, the Sun, and other bodies -- continuously for billions of years, without producing any observed catastrophe.

titles.text: The cosmic-ray natural experiment

phrasings.text: Because Nature has already carried out particle collisions at LHC-and-higher energies far more often than the LHC ever could, without any resulting catastrophe, LHC collisions introduce no novel danger relative to what Earth and other astronomical bodies have already survived.

titles.text: Cosmic-ray flux bounds LHC-collision risk

phrasings.text: The natural-experiment argument alone leaves one loophole open: a collider-produced black hole could be slow-moving and gravitationally captured by Earth, whereas a cosmic-ray-produced black hole is ultra-relativistic and would pass through Earth without being captured, so cosmic rays' harmlessness does not by itself establish that a slow, captured black hole would also be harmless.

titles.text: Loophole: slow captured black holes vs fast cosmic rays

phrasings.text: Giddings and Mangano analyzed the hypothetical worst case in which a TeV-scale black hole is stable and becomes gravitationally trapped, building accretion models from first-principles, well-tested physics across a wide range of TeV-gravity scenarios.

source.title: Giddings & Mangano, Astrophysical implications of hypothetical stable TeV-scale black holes, 2008; Phys. Rev. D 78, 035009

titles.text: Giddings & Mangano: worst-case stable trapped black hole

phrasings.text: Giddings and Mangano identify specific observed white dwarf stars whose mass, magnetic field, and age make them living evidence that stable, dangerous micro black holes cannot be produced, because such stars would otherwise have been destroyed.

source.title: Giddings & Mangano, Astrophysical implications of hypothetical stable TeV-scale black holes, 2008; Phys. Rev. D 78, 035009

titles.text: Specific white dwarfs as living evidence

demoted.reason: Popular reporting (s17) restating the Giddings-Mangano white-dwarf result already carried by the primary study n031 (s02); a secondary aggregator is not the source of the factual claim. n034 stays grounded by n031/n030/n033.

phrasings.text: Popular reporting summarized the Giddings-Mangano result as showing that certain white dwarf stars are dense enough to trap a slow, stable micro black hole if one existed, so the continued existence of those stars proves such black holes are not produced.

source.title: Physicists Rule Out the Production of Dangerous Black Holes at the LHC — Phys.org, 2008

titles.text: Phys.org: dense white dwarfs would have trapped such a black hole

phrasings.text: In presenting the LSAG findings, Ellis laid out a layered safety case in which, even if a micro black hole were hypothetically stable, the cosmic-ray natural experiment plus the survival of white dwarfs and neutron stars bounds the risk to negligible.

source.title: John Ellis, 'The LHC is Safe', CERN Colloquium, 14 August 2008 (slides)

titles.text: Ellis: layered case includes stellar-survival backstop

phrasings.text: The observed survival of dense white dwarf and neutron stars closes the slow-black-hole loophole: a slow, stable, dangerous black hole produced when a cosmic ray struck one of these far denser, older stars would have been gravitationally captured and would have gone on to destroy it, so those stars' continued existence is direct evidence that such black holes are not produced by LHC-energy collisions either.

titles.text: Stellar survival closes the slow-black-hole loophole

phrasings.text: Giddings and Mangano note that neutron stars alone are less decisive than white dwarfs for some scenarios because their strong magnetic fields can decelerate incoming cosmic rays enough to lower the effective collision energy below the LHC range, which is why the white-dwarf argument carries the main evidentiary load.

source.title: Giddings & Mangano, Astrophysical implications of hypothetical stable TeV-scale black holes, 2008; Phys. Rev. D 78, 035009

titles.text: Neutron-star magnetic fields decelerate cosmic rays

phrasings.text: Neutron stars carry less evidentiary weight than white dwarfs in the stellar-survival bound, because a neutron star's strong magnetic field can decelerate incoming cosmic rays enough to drop the effective collision energy below the LHC range.

titles.text: White dwarfs, not neutron stars, carry the main load

phrasings.text: Giddings and Mangano's accretion analysis bounds the growth rate of a hypothetical captured black hole well below the Bondi accretion rate and shows it is further suppressed quantum-mechanically at subatomic scales, giving accretion timescales for any macroscopic effect longer than the Sun's remaining lifetime.

source.title: Giddings & Mangano, Astrophysical implications of hypothetical stable TeV-scale black holes, 2008; Phys. Rev. D 78, 035009

titles.text: Giddings & Mangano: accretion bounded well below Bondi rate

phrasings.text: In a follow-up rebutting specific metastable-black-hole claims, Giddings and Mangano reaffirm that the accretion rate for a captured black hole is bounded well below the Bondi accretion rate and further suppressed quantum-mechanically at subatomic scales, so no macroscopic growth occurs within the lifetime of the solar system.

source.title: Giddings & Mangano, Comments on claimed risk from metastable black holes, 2008

titles.text: Giddings & Mangano rebuttal: accretion still too slow

phrasings.text: Even in the scenario where a stable black hole is gravitationally captured by Earth, its accretion of surrounding matter would be far too slow to matter within any relevant timescale: the rate is bounded well below the Bondi accretion rate and further suppressed quantum-mechanically at subatomic scales, giving an accretion timescale longer than the solar system's remaining lifetime.

titles.text: Captured black hole would accrete too slowly to matter

phrasings.text: Giddings and Mangano directly rebutted specific counter-claims (associated with Rossler and others) that micro black holes could be metastable and dangerous, showing those scenarios either contradict well-tested physics or are already excluded by the same astrophysical white-dwarf/neutron-star bounds.

source.title: Giddings & Mangano, Comments on claimed risk from metastable black holes, 2008

titles.text: Giddings & Mangano rebut specific metastable-black-hole claims

phrasings.text: Specific contrarian claims that collider-produced black holes could be metastable and dangerous do not survive scrutiny: they either contradict well-established, well-tested physics or are already excluded by the same white-dwarf/neutron-star astrophysical bounds.

titles.text: Metastable-black-hole risk claims are excluded, not just disputed

phrasings.text: Ellis emphasized that the cosmic-ray argument rests on collisions Nature has already performed at energies equal to or exceeding the LHC's, so the LHC introduces no novel danger.

source.title: John Ellis, 'The LHC is Safe', CERN Colloquium, 14 August 2008 (slides)

titles.text: Ellis: LHC introduces no novel danger vs Nature's collisions

demoted.reason: Popular reporting (s17) narrating the layered safety case (Hawking evaporation + white-dwarf backstop) already carried by primary nodes n015 and n031/n034 and the LSAG node n021; secondary aggregator, not a source of fact. n022 keeps 6 grounds.

phrasings.text: Reporting on the Giddings-Mangano result noted that the first line of defense remains that black holes evaporate via Hawking radiation, with the white-dwarf/neutron-star argument serving as an independent backstop for the hypothetical no-evaporation case.

source.title: Physicists Rule Out the Production of Dangerous Black Holes at the LHC — Phys.org, 2008

titles.text: Phys.org: white-dwarf argument as backstop, not first line

phrasings.text: CMS and ATLAS microscopic-black-hole searches have used LHC proton-proton collisions at successively higher center-of-mass energies -- 7 TeV in the earliest search, then 8 TeV, then 13 TeV in later runs.

source.title: CMS Collaboration, Search for Microscopic Black Hole Signatures at the Large Hadron Collider, 2011; Phys. Lett. B 697, 434

titles.text: LHC's actual collision energies: 7, 8, then 13 TeV

phrasings.text: LHC microscopic-black-hole searches through the 8 TeV and 13 TeV runs found no black-hole signal across on the order of 10^15 total proton-proton collisions analyzed.

source.title: CMS Collaboration, Search for Microscopic Black Hole Signatures at the Large Hadron Collider, 2011; Phys. Lett. B 697, 434

titles.text: ~10^15 LHC collisions analyzed, no black-hole signal

phrasings.text: The LHC's total collision sample over its operating lifetime, though large by laboratory standards (on the order of 10^15 proton-proton collisions), is many orders of magnitude smaller than the number of comparable- or higher-energy collisions nature has already produced via cosmic rays striking Earth alone (on the order of 10^22).

titles.text: LHC's collision total is tiny next to nature's cosmic-ray total

phrasings.text: The LHC is not a uniquely powerful or unprecedented high-energy environment: its per-collision energies and its total collision statistics both fall far short of, respectively, the energy scale standard gravity requires for black-hole production and the scale of high-energy collisions nature routinely supplies via cosmic rays.

titles.text: The LHC is not a uniquely powerful environment

phrasings.text: CMS's first search for microscopic-black-hole signatures analyzed LHC collision events with large total transverse energy and multiple high-energy jets, leptons, and photons, and found them in good agreement with Standard Model expectations, dominated by ordinary QCD multijet production, with no black-hole signal seen.

source.title: CMS Collaboration, Search for Microscopic Black Hole Signatures at the Large Hadron Collider, 2011; Phys. Lett. B 697, 434

titles.text: LHC collisions match ordinary Standard Model (QCD) events

phrasings.text: A CMS search of 7 TeV LHC proton-proton collisions found no excess of high-multiplicity, high-transverse-energy events over Standard Model background expectations, providing no evidence of microscopic-black-hole production.

source.title: CMS Collaboration, Search for microscopic black holes in pp collisions at sqrt(s) = 7 TeV, 2012; JHEP 04 (2012) 061

titles.text: CMS 7 TeV search: no excess over Standard Model background

phrasings.text: LHC proton-proton collisions actually produce, overwhelmingly, showers of ordinary Standard Model particles via known QCD and electroweak processes -- not black holes or other exotic new objects.

titles.text: LHC collisions actually produce ordinary particle showers

phrasings.text: A CMS search of 7 TeV LHC data excluded semiclassical microscopic-black-hole production below minimum masses of roughly 4-6 TeV, across a range of extra-dimension model parameters, finding no signal beyond Standard Model expectations.

source.title: CMS Collaboration, Search for microscopic black holes in pp collisions at sqrt(s) = 7 TeV, 2012; JHEP 04 (2012) 061

titles.text: CMS 7 TeV exclusion: ~4-6 TeV mass range

phrasings.text: CMS's first search for microscopic-black-hole signatures set lower mass-exclusion limits of about 3.5-4.5 TeV using early 7 TeV LHC data; subsequent higher-energy (8 and 13 TeV) CMS and ATLAS searches extended these exclusions to roughly 5-11 TeV, depending on the model.

source.title: CMS Collaboration, Search for Microscopic Black Hole Signatures at the Large Hadron Collider, 2011; Phys. Lett. B 697, 434

titles.text: LHC exclusion limits rise with energy: 3.5 to 11 TeV

phrasings.text: The rising LHC exclusion limits on black-hole production, from a few TeV up into the 5-11 TeV range across successive, higher-energy searches, show that if a low fundamental Planck scale exists at all, it must lie above everything the LHC has directly probed so far, pushing the speculative production scenario further from any experimental support with each higher-energy run.

titles.text: Rising exclusion limits push production further from support

phrasings.text: LHC exclusion limits on black-hole production do not by themselves establish safety, because they constrain only specific semiclassical production models at currently-probed energies, leaving open unprobed parameter space (a higher fundamental Planck scale) that current searches cannot rule out.

titles.text: Exclusion limits alone don't prove safety

phrasings.text: CERN's LHC Safety Assessment Group (LSAG) -- John Ellis, Gian Giudice, Michelangelo Mangano, Igor Tkachev and Urs Wiedemann -- concluded in its 2008 review that 'LHC collisions present no danger and that there are no reasons for concern,' reaffirming and extending an earlier 2003 safety review.

source.title: Review of the Safety of LHC Collisions (LHC Safety Assessment Group: Ellis, Giudice, Mangano, Tkachev, Wiedemann), 2008; publ. J. Phys. G 35, 115004

titles.text: LSAG's formal verdict: no danger, no reasons for concern

phrasings.text: The LHC's safety rests on an explicit, formal, unambiguous verdict issued by a named panel of physicists specifically tasked with assessing the risk, not merely on an absence of public objection or an informal assumption of safety.

titles.text: Safety is a formal reviewed verdict, not an assumption

phrasings.text: LSAG's review assessed all four hypothetical catastrophe scenarios raised publicly about the LHC -- microscopic black holes, strangelets, vacuum bubbles, and magnetic monopoles -- and found no conceivable danger from any of them at LHC energies.

source.title: Review of the Safety of LHC Collisions (LHC Safety Assessment Group: Ellis, Giudice, Mangano, Tkachev, Wiedemann), 2008; publ. J. Phys. G 35, 115004

titles.text: LSAG assessed all four scenarios, not just black holes

phrasings.text: LSAG's black-hole conclusion is not an isolated, narrowly-tailored finding: the same review applied comparable scrutiny to three other independent categories of hypothetical catastrophe and reached the same no-danger verdict for each, evidence against the review being selectively reasoned toward a predetermined answer on black holes alone.

titles.text: Consistent verdict across scenario types argues against selective reasoning

phrasings.text: Walter Wagner (a retired nuclear-safety officer) and Luis Sancho (a Spanish science writer) filed suit in the U.S. District Court in Hawaii in March 2008 seeking to delay LHC start-up for a renewed safety review, naming the U.S. Department of Energy, Fermilab, the National Science Foundation and CERN.

source.title: Doomsday fears spark lawsuit over collider — NBC News (Cosmic Log), 2008

titles.text: The 2008 Wagner-Sancho lawsuit

phrasings.text: The Wagner-Sancho lawsuit's central scenario was that a microscopic black hole produced at the LHC might grow by consuming Earth; the suit additionally raised three other scenarios (strangelets converting normal matter, magnetic monopoles catalyzing proton decay, and vacuum bubbles) as further grounds for delay.

source.title: Doomsday fears spark lawsuit over collider — NBC News (Cosmic Log), 2008

titles.text: The lawsuit's black-hole-growth scenario

phrasings.text: The Wagner-Sancho lawsuit is documented as a legal and public-communication challenge to LHC operations, not a peer-reviewed scientific refutation of the safety case.

source.title: Doomsday fears spark lawsuit over collider — NBC News (Cosmic Log), 2008

titles.text: A legal challenge, not a peer-reviewed refutation

phrasings.text: The contrarian catastrophe position's most prominent public challenge has never gone through the peer-reviewed scientific process that produced the safety case (LSAG, the RHIC review, Giddings & Mangano) -- it was a legal filing, not a scientific paper, which is a real asymmetry in the evidentiary quality behind the two positions.

titles.text: The contrarian challenge bypassed peer review; the safety case did not

phrasings.text: The Wagner-Sancho lawsuit was dismissed: the court held it lacked jurisdiction over the LHC (the U.S. does not control CERN's operations) and, on appeal, that the plaintiffs had failed to show a credible threat of harm.

source.title: LHC lawsuit case dismissed by US court — Phys.org, 2010

titles.text: Lawsuit dismissed: no jurisdiction, no credible threat shown

phrasings.text: During the appeal, a friend-of-the-court brief from physicists including Richard Wilson and Nobel laureates Sheldon Glashow and Frank Wilczek argued the doomsday scenarios had no realistic chance of occurring.

source.title: LHC lawsuit case dismissed by US court — Phys.org, 2010

titles.text: Amicus brief: Glashow, Wilczek and Wilson find no realistic chance

phrasings.text: Reporting on the lawsuit's final dismissal noted it turned on the plaintiffs' failure to demonstrate a credible, imminent threat of harm, consistent with the scientific consensus that the collisions are safe.

source.title: LHC lawsuit dismissed by US court — Symmetry Magazine, 2010

titles.text: Dismissal reporting: failure to show imminent threat

phrasings.text: The lawsuit's catastrophe scenarios did not withstand outside scrutiny either: a U.S. court found the plaintiffs failed to show a credible threat of harm, and independent physicists outside CERN's own review process separately concluded, in a sworn brief, that the scenarios had no realistic chance of occurring.

titles.text: The lawsuit's scenarios failed under legal and independent scrutiny too

phrasings.text: Multiple institutionally-independent reviews -- CERN's own LSAG expert panel, the earlier peer-reviewed RHIC disaster-scenario analysis, and the outside physicists' assessment submitted during the LHC lawsuit's judicial review -- converge on the same no-danger conclusion using comparable cosmic-ray-based reasoning, which is stronger evidence than any single review standing alone.

titles.text: Independent expert reviews converge on no danger

phrasings.text: A microscopic black hole produced at the LHC that failed to evaporate via Hawking radiation and was gravitationally captured by Earth could slowly accrete surrounding matter and, over a sufficiently long time, grow large enough to threaten Earth.

titles.text: Steelman: a stable, captured micro black hole could grow and threaten Earth

phrasings.text: The safety conclusion against LHC-produced black holes does not depend on the collider exclusion-limit searches establishing safety on their own, because the independent astrophysical white-dwarf/neutron-star bound already constrains the risk regardless of what parameter space those searches have or have not directly probed.

titles.text: Empirical layer isn't load-bearing either -- astrophysical bound covers the gap

phrasings.text: Direct operation of the LHC has produced no black holes: its collisions yield only ordinary Standard-Model particle showers, and dedicated searches have found no black-hole signature.

titles.text: The operating LHC has produced no black holes
